I think any polynomial interpolation rule will have this property that there are at most ```len(self.waves)``` distinct terms that are being summed, with well-defined coefficients.

To be honest, the reason I did it this way was that the code seemed straightforward, and I thought there was no way that this was the limiting step.  Instantiation of InterpolatedImages seemed like it should be more expensive.  To test out this gut feeling, I just wrote a script that draws the ChromaticOpticalPSF from the unit tests, but with differently thinned versions of the LSST r bandpass that have a different ```len(bandpass.wave_list)```.  For ```len(bandpass.wave_list)``` = 23, 54, 73, the time to render a PSF image (not convolved with galaxy) was typically 0.5, 0.75, and 1.7 seconds.  That is a kind of weird scaling that I'm not quite sure how to interpret - I'm guessing its the sum of a nearly constant overhead (for the instantiation of the InterpolatedImage that always happens before drawing) plus a term that scales with the length of the combined wave list.  But anyway, it seems you're right - this is more important than I was expecting and I think I should refactor the code to account for it.

The way this would work (I think) is that I would have a subroutine that does the steps in ```_imageAtWavelength``` that find ```lower_idx``` and ```frac``` to interpolate between two stored images.  Using that subroutine here as well, I could figure out (for each wavelength of the combined wave list) which of the stored images contribute to the integral, and with what relative weights.  Then I could just take the total weighted sum, without calling any kind of ```DirectImageIntegrator``` at all.  (I suppose I could leave that new class just in case someone wants to implement a version of the integration that doesn't just use linear/polynomial interpolation between the stored images. But I don't need it if I do what you suggested, I think.)

So, the most basic form of that is hard, because acquiring a read lock and then "upgrading" it to a write lock is a rare, and fairly restrictive operation. The `RwLock` in the stdlib does not allow for it, and although the `RwLock` in the `parking_lot` crate does, you need to actually acquire explicitly as `upgradeable_read`, which is exclusive (just like acquiring for write).

I've also implemented a tactic involving adding a "mutation generation" to the graph that allowed us to cycle detect for a particular generation (not the same "generation" concept as on #6059) under the read lock, and then skip cycle detection if we acquired the write lock and the generation hadn't changed. And unfortunately that was _also_ not faster.

But this comment is sortof fud, because the most time I've ever seen cycle detection take in a profile is about 3%.

I could see advantages in that, yeah -- for example, then `user_can_write_object` could be called directly, externally. Except that's also a disadvantage -- since `user_can_write_object` wouldn't always be there, we couldn't ever actually safely call it in an agnostic way, since the method wouldn't always be defined. And even if it were always there, we'd never want to call it externally since we may have overridden the more specific permission method.

If it feels awkward having a method on the base class that won't always get used, perhaps we just leave it out entirely, have everything return `False` by default, and allow subclasses to define their own DRY way of lumping multiple permission types together, as appropriate?

I just realized we may need to be careful here. From the `typing` description on PyPI:

> NOTE: in Python 3.5 and later, the typing module lives in the stdlib, and installing this package has NO EFFECT. To get a newer version of the typing module in Python 3.5 or later, you have to upgrade to a newer Python (bugfix) version. For example, typing in Python 3.6.0 is missing the definition of ‘Type’ – upgrading to 3.6.2 will fix this.

Even though this fix is included in the latest version of `typing`, it may not be included in every version of Python 3.5+ installed by devs from their OS package manager. If we think this is the case, we should probably leave this ignore. Hopefully we don't hit too many issues like this where we have to work around old versions of `typing` to allow testing to work consistently in Travis and different dev environments.

First, the notion of "local" scheduler is being outdated with this rewrite, because there's no "global" scheduler, which make the term ambiguous and suggestive of the fact there's something other than the "local" scheduler. Second, I think people might simply be used to the term and, naturally, have affinity for calling this component as it was named before. I would encourage to take a step back and think of what this component is actually doing. Which leads to (3) scheduling is only part of the NodeManager's responsibility. In fact, the scheduler is a component inside the NodeManager, periodically invoked to make scheduling decisions. I felt like the NodeManager is more appropriate, as it is more general and descriptive of the fact that this component does more than just scheduling. It performs functions related to the management of the local node. Raylet is really the name of the whole process that includes node management, object management, etc.

Let's not reinvent the wheel here and follow what others do. Getters and setters should be named like variables, see https://github.com/apache/arrow/blob/master/cpp/src/arrow/array.h#L224

This is what Arrow does, what TensorFlow does [1], and even the C++ standard does it that way. It's also mentioned in the Google style guide.

[1] https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/cc/framework/ops.h#L42
